title: Chapter 26: Descent
content: I went at midnight.

Not because darkness offered cover — the Hollow existed outside such distinctions — but because the tide forced my hand. The Drowned Church only surfaced at low tide, and the low came at twelve-seventeen a.m. according to the almanac I'd found wedged behind the circulation desk at the Blackwood Vale Public Library, its pages swollen with decades of damp.

I walked the marsh path alone. The same path Nadine must have walked. The same path Isobel walked, and Marta, and Carlota, and the others whose names I'd learned by heart and then by blood. The boardwalk ended at the reed line, and after that it was just mud and memory, my shoes sinking into the cold black muck with every step, the chill seeping through the soles and into my bones until I couldn't tell where my feet ended and the marsh began.
Then I saw it.

The Drowned Church rose from the black water like a skeleton. Just the frame of it — the steeple snapped and listing, the walls half-collapsed, the windows eyeless sockets staring at nothing. Two centuries it had sat in this marsh, swallowed and half-regurgitated by the tide twice daily. The stone was black with algae and age. The wood was grey and soft as rotting teeth. And the smell — God, the smell. Stagnant water and old stone and something beneath both, something organic and patient, like the breath of a thing that had been waiting a very long time to exhale.

I waded through the shallows. The water was so cold it burned, then went numb, then became just another sensation I had to endure to get where I was going. That seemed to be the theme of my life lately.

The church door hung on one hinge. I pushed it and it groaned — the first sound I'd heard since leaving the road. Inside, the silence was absolute. Not the silence of empty space, but the silence of a held breath. Of something listening. I could hear my own heartbeat, loud and ridiculous, the drum of a living thing announcing itself to the dead.
The altar was half-buried in silt. Behind it, a crack in the floor opened into darkness. Not the darkness of absence but of presence — a darkness that was waiting to be entered.
I climbed down.
The Hollow was not dark.

That's what I hadn't expected. I'd prepared for darkness. For some underworld gloom, some cavernous shadow. But the Hollow was grey. Grey the way a fog is grey, except this fog had architecture. It had walls — or rather, it had boundaries that functioned as walls, vertical planes of luminous vapour that gave the impression of a room without actually being solid. The floor was level beneath my feet but felt tilted, as though gravity itself was being negotiated here, terms still under discussion.

I stood in a corridor of grey. Fog-walls on either side, narrowing ahead into what looked like a chamber. The air tasted like ash — dry, mineral, the residue of something that had been burned long ago and never quite stopped smoldering. I swallowed and the taste stuck to the back of my throat, fine and choking, like compliance. Like every time I'd ever swallowed a truth because speaking it would have been inconvenient for someone else.
I walked forward. The corridor opened into a room.

A table sat at the centre. Older than the town — older, probably, than the country. The wood was dark and dense, carved with names that glowed faintly in the grey light, each letter limned with a pale luminescence that pulsed like a slow heartbeat. I recognized some of the names. Buena. I saw it repeated, generation after generation, carved into the same wood alongside others I didn't know. The table smelled of old contracts and fresh grief, ink and tears, the particular musk of bargains struck under duress.
The bureaucracy of suffering. An office building in hell, with better interior design.
Around the table, seven figures.

They were not monstrous. That was the worst part. They were simply… present. Grey-robed, faceless — not in the way that horror movies are faceless, with hollow eye sockets and screaming maws, but faceless the way a blank form is faceless. A template. A document waiting to be filled in. They had the shapes of people without the particulars, and their stillness was more unnerving than any threat could have been.
They didn't attack. They waited.

One of them extended a hand. Not threatening. Inviting. The way a colleague offers a handshake. The way a court officer shows you to your seat. With the casual authority of something that has done this many, many times before.
"Aurelia Buena. The seventh."

The voice came from everywhere and nowhere. It had no gender, no accent, no texture whatsoever. It was like dry paper being read aloud in an empty room. A voice without features — the voice of a process, not a person.
"We've been expecting you."

I said nothing. In my old life — the life before Blackwood Vale, before the contract, before any of this — I'd been taught that silence was a weapon. Let the other side fill the void. Let them show their hand.
The Council did not need to fill the void. The void was their medium.

"We offer you a deal," the paper-voice continued. "Submit. Let the contract complete. And the next generation will be spared. No more Buena women. No more curse. Just—" a pause, almost gentle, "—ending."
The word hung in the grey air like a judgement.
Ending.

I felt it land in my chest. Not as a threat — as a promise. Ending. The word I'd been too afraid to want. No more nights lying awake, feeling the curse move beneath my skin like something alive. No more watching the women I loved disappear piece by piece into something that wore their faces. No more fighting a war that had already consumed six generations.
Just ending.

The exhaustion was real. I was so tired. Tired the way a bone is tired after it's been broken and reset and broken again. Tired the way a field is tired after too many harvests. Six generations of fighting — Nadine, who had documented everything and still been consumed; Isobel, who had run and been found; Marta, who had bargained and been cheated; Carlota, who had prayed and been ignored — and every single one of them had thought, at some point, in some dark and private hour: *What if I just stopped? What if I just let it end?*

I had thought it too. More than once. On the nights when the fear sat on my chest like a second skeleton and the only voice in my head was the one that whispered *you can't keep doing this.* The deal would end it. Not just for me — for everyone. Eternal peace through surrender. The most seductive lie ever told.
For three full breaths, I considered it.

Three breaths. In the grey room, surrounded by the faceless and the formless, with the taste of ash thick on my tongue and the weight of every Buena woman who had come before me pressing down on my shoulders, I let myself want it. I let myself imagine what it would feel like to stop. To close the file. To lay down six generations of briefs and just say *fine. Fine. You win.*

And then I heard Nadine's voice — not from the grey, but from memory. From the journal entries I'd read until the pages went soft. *They moved the terms, Aurelia. Every time we met them, they moved the terms.*
My head came up.
"No more Buena women," I repeated. My voice sounded small in the grey. Papery too — but different. Alive. "That's what you said. No more Buena women. No more curse."
"Yes," the Council said, and I could almost hear the satisfaction in the paper-voice. Almost.

"But 'no more Buena women' doesn't mean 'no more curse.'" The words came faster now, the way they always did in court when I'd found the thread and started pulling. "It means the curse moves on. To someone else. You don't end curses — you transfer them. I've read the contract. I've read every version of it, every codicil, every amendment scrawled in the margins by women who were trying to protect their daughters. Bad faith. All of it."
The Council did not move. The grey did not change. But something in the room shifted — a pressure, a sense of attention refocusing, like a judge looking up from their notes.

"Every generation, you moved the goalposts," I said. "Every woman who fought, you called it consent. Every boundary she set, you called it an invitation. Marta bargained for her daughter's freedom and you took her silence as assent. Carlota prayed for escape and you called her devotion submission. Nadine documented every clause and you called her research acceptance." I was pacing now, circling the table, the names glowing faintly beneath my fingertips as I trailed my hand across the ancient wood. "You turned resistance into agreement. You turned suffering into signature. You turned *no* into *yes* because it was more convenient for your paperwork."
"You are emotional," the Council said. Not dismissive — flat. Procedural. "This does not change the terms."

"I'm not trying to change the terms. I'm trying to expose them." I stopped pacing. I turned to face the seven faceless figures, and I felt the ash-taste rise in my throat again, and I swallowed it, and I spoke through the grit of it. "You offered me ending. But ending isn't what you sell. You sell transfer. You sell the same deal to the next family, and the next, and the next, world without end. The Buena line closes and you find new vessels. New names on your table. New women to farm. That's the contract. That's *your* consent — mine. You need me to say yes so you can keep going. So you can keep *feeding.*"
The silence that followed was different from the silence before. Thicker. The grey walls seemed to lean in, just slightly, the way walls do when they're about to close.
"Then you refuse," the Council said.
"I refuse."
And then they attacked.
Not with hands. Not with claws or teeth or anything so honest as violence. They attacked with weight.

It came from everywhere at once — from the grey walls, from the table of names, from the floor that felt tilted and the ceiling that wasn't there. The accumulated compliance of six generations pressing down on me like a hand on my chest. Every time a Buena woman had said *yes* when she meant *no.* Every time she had stayed when she should have left. Every time she had been silent when she should have screamed. All of it — 260 years of it — descended on my shoulders and mine was not a back built to carry the weight of centuries.

My knees buckled. The floor met me — cold and level and tilted, all at once, and the weight increased. I felt Carlota's prayers pressing down, all that devotion turned to obligation. I felt Marta's bargains, every concession she'd made in good faith, every compromise that had been exploited. I felt Isobel's running — the way she'd fled halfway across the country and still been found, still been dragged back, still been told she had *agreed* to the return. I felt Nadine's documentation, every careful note she'd made, and the way the Council had read her diligence as participation, her awareness as acceptance.

And beneath it all, older and heavier than any of them, I felt the original contract. The first Buena woman — the one whose name I had never found, the one who had been erased from history the way women always are — and her original *yes,* spoken in a language that might not even have been English, under circumstances I could not imagine, in a world that gave her no other choice.

Her compliance was the heaviest thing of all. Because it had been real. Because she had said yes — not because she wanted to, but because she'd had no other word. Because the world she lived in had never taught her *no.*

I was on my hands and knees. The weight was pressing me down — not just physically, but existentially. The weight of every woman who had ever gone along to get along. Every woman who had ever swallowed her rage because expressing it would have cost too much. Every woman who had ever said *I'm fine* when she wasn't.
The taste of ash flooded my mouth. I was choking on it — on all of it — on 260 years of swallowed truth.
I thought: *this is how it ends. Not with a fight. With a surrender so old it feels like gravity.*
And then I thought: *no.*
Not the *no* of defiance. Not the *no* of heroism. Just the *no* of a woman who has been pressed flat and decides, for no reason except that she can, to get up.

I pushed. Not against the Council — against the weight. Against every compliance I'd ever performed and every silence I'd ever kept and every *I'm fine* I'd ever said through gritted teeth. I pushed with everything I had, which wasn't much, which was barely enough, which was just enough.
The grey walls shuddered.

I crawled. Hand over hand, across the tilted floor, toward the corridor I'd come through. The weight on my back was enormous — I could feel my spine compressing, my shoulders curling, my body trying to fold into the shape of a woman who accepts what is given. I refused. I kept crawling.

The corridor was longer going out than coming in. Or maybe I was slower. The grey pressed in on every side and I could feel the Council behind me, not pursuing, not needing to pursue — just waiting, with the patience of bureaucrats who had done this six times before and could do it six hundred more.
I found the crack in the floor. I climbed.
The Drowned Church was filling with water.

The tide was coming in — I could hear it now, the black water rushing through the broken door, rising around my legs as I hauled myself up through the crack and onto the silted floor of the nave. Cold. So cold it knocked the breath out of me. But the cold was real — not the grey non-temperature of the Hollow but the honest cold of Blackwood Vale's marsh water, and I would have wept with relief if I'd had the air for it.

I waded through the church. The water rose to my thighs, then my waist. The door was half-submerged. I went through it sideways, the current pulling at me, the tide determined to reclaim what it had given up.

I staggered through the shallows. Fell. Got up. Fell again. The marsh grass was slick and sharp under my hands. I crawled the last twenty feet on my hands and knees, the water draining from my clothes, the taste of ash still bitter on my tongue, my pulse thudding in my ears like a judge's gavel.
I collapsed onto the marsh grass and lay there, gasping, staring up at the stars.
They were real too. Not grey. Not weightless. Just stars, cold and distant and indifferent to everything that had happened beneath them.

My hands were shaking. My whole body was shaking. I lay in the wet grass and let the tremors come and felt the weight of 260 years slowly, slowly lift — not gone, never gone, just redistributed, back into the bones of the women who'd carried it before me, back into the table with the glowing names, back into the grey room beneath the Drowned Church where seven faceless figures were already preparing for the next negotiation.
I pulled out my journal. The pages were damp but the pen worked.

*They tried to bury me under 260 years of compliance. Every woman who ever stayed too long. Every woman who ever doubted herself. Every woman who ever said 'I'm fine' when she wasn't. They tried to make me one of them.*
*They almost succeeded.*

I closed the journal. The tide was still rising behind me. The Drowned Church was sinking back into the black water, its steeple disappearing inch by inch, and I knew — with the terrible certainty of someone who had just sat across the table from the enemy and seen how they operated — that this was not over. The Council would not let me go. Not because I'd refused — because I'd *understood.* I'd read the contract. I'd found the bad faith. I'd litigated.
And now they knew I could fight them on their own terms.
I lay in the grass and listened to my heartbeat and felt the weight still pressing, faint but persistent, like a hand on my shoulder, like a voice in my ear, like a contract waiting to be signed.
The tide kept rising.
